description: C# / .NET baseline invariants — deliberately narrow, only patterns that are nearly always a vulnerability: insecure Json.NET TypeNameHandling (deserialization RCE), BinaryFormatter, SQL built by string concatenation into SqlCommand/CommandText, a TLS certificate-validation callback hardcoded to accept everything, and Process.Start with a concatenated command line. Each rule anchors on a concrete .NET API so generic C# code does not trip it.

rules.//: Json.NET (Newtonsoft) honors $type from the payload when TypeNameHandling is anything other than None, letting an attacker instantiate arbitrary types during deserialization — a well-known RCE gadget chain. Only None is safe. The optional `TypeNameHandling.` qualifier covers both `TypeNameHandling = TypeNameHandling.Auto` (object-initializer) and a bare `= Auto`. None is excluded by enumerating only the dangerous members.
rules.message: Json.NET TypeNameHandling set to All/Auto/Objects/Arrays embeds CLR type names in the payload and instantiates them on read — a deserialization RCE vector. Use TypeNameHandling.None and a strongly-typed model, or a vetted ISerializationBinder if polymorphism is unavoidable.

rules.//: BinaryFormatter deserializes attacker-controlled byte streams into live objects via type metadata in the stream — Microsoft has formally deprecated it as insecure with no safe usage. Any `new BinaryFormatter(` is a finding. Replace with System.Text.Json / a contract-based serializer (DataContractSerializer with known types, protobuf, MessagePack).
rules.message: BinaryFormatter is insecure-by-design (deserialization RCE) and deprecated by Microsoft — use System.Text.Json, or DataContractSerializer/protobuf/MessagePack with a known-types allowlist. There is no safe way to BinaryFormatter.Deserialize untrusted input.

rules.//: SQL assembled by `+` concatenation and handed to ADO.NET is classic SQL injection. Two shapes are caught: `new SqlCommand("..." + var)` (constructor) and `cmd.CommandText = "..." + var` (property). The literal must be immediately followed by `\s*\+`, so a parameterized query `new SqlCommand("... WHERE id = @id", conn)` (closing quote followed by `,`) does NOT match. Use SqlParameter / @named bound parameters, never string building.
rules.message: SQL built by string concatenation passed to SqlCommand/CommandText — a SQL injection sink. Use parameterized queries: `new SqlCommand("... WHERE id = @id", conn)` with `cmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@id", id)` (or a typed SqlParameter).

rules.//: Assigning a certificate-validation callback whose body is `=> true` accepts ANY server certificate, defeating TLS and enabling a trivial MITM. The optional `Custom` infix covers both ServicePointManager.ServerCertificateValidationCallback and HttpClientHandler.ServerCertificateCustomValidationCallback. `\+?=` catches both `=` and `+=`; `[^;\n]*` keeps the match on the lambda parameter list so the `=> true` body is the validator return, not an unrelated later statement. A real validator that inspects chain/errors will not end in a bare `=> true`.
rules.message: TLS certificate-validation callback hardcoded to return true accepts any certificate — a man-in-the-middle bypass. Validate the chain and SslPolicyErrors (return errors == SslPolicyErrors.None), or pin the expected cert/CA. For a local self-signed dev server, scope the trust narrowly and never ship it.

rules.//: Process.Start with a concatenated first argument splices a runtime value into the command/path string. When that value is user-influenced it is command/argument injection. WARN (not HARD) because Process.Start has overloads (filename, ProcessStartInfo) where concatenation of a trusted constant is benign, and the line-level regex cannot prove the source. Fix: pass arguments via ProcessStartInfo.ArgumentList (no shell, no quoting), never a concatenated command line.
rules.message: Process.Start with a concatenated command line interpolates a runtime value into the invocation — argument/command injection if user-influenced. Use ProcessStartInfo with ArgumentList (each arg added separately, no shell) instead of building one string.
